metadata.name: py
metadata.description: Sidex attribute schemas for the Python codegen target.

metadata.name: json
metadata.description: Sidex attribute schemas for the JSON codegen target.

metadata.name: rust
metadata.description: Sidex attribute schemas for the Rust codegen target.

metadata.name: validate
metadata.description: Sidex attribute schemas for the validation extension.

metadata.name: api
metadata.description: Sidex attribute schemas for API stability and lifecycle attributes.

metadata.name: rpc_attrs
metadata.description: Sidex attribute schemas for RPC-style request / response declarations.

docs.text:
Meta-attributes for declaring typed attribute schemas.

A plugin defines its accepted attributes as ordinary Sidex defs annotated
with `#[attrs(plugin = "<id>", target = "<position>")]`. The compiler
discovers these defs at IR build time, validates the source attributes
against them, and stores the parsed result on the corresponding IR node's
`typed_attrs` field. Plugin codegens then deserialize that value into a
generated, typed Rust struct without re-running attribute parsing.

Sidex builtin types.

This schema is used to populate the builtin types.

Typed attributes for the Python codegen target.

Plugins use these schemas to drive code generation; the compiler parses
source `#[py(...)]` attributes against them and stores the result in the
IR's `typed_attrs["py"]` field. Downstream codegens deserialize that
value directly into the generated Rust types via `serde_json::from_value`.

Typed attributes for the JSON codegen target.

Plugins use these schemas to drive code generation; the compiler parses
source `#[json(...)]` attributes against them and stores the result in
the IR's `typed_attrs["json"]` map. Downstream codegens deserialize
that value directly into the generated Rust types via
`serde_json::from_value`.

Each record is annotated `#[json(rename_all = "snake_case")]` so that
the generated deserializers expect JSON keys matching the parser's
emit shape (the parser writes Sidex field names verbatim, which are
already in `snake_case`).

Typed attributes for the Rust codegen target.

Source `#[rust(...)]` attributes are validated against these schemas at
IR build time and stored on the IR's `typed_attrs["rust"]` map. Each
record uses `#[json(rename_all = "snake_case")]` so wire keys match the
parser's emit shape.

Typed attributes for the validation extension.

Each `#[validate({ … }, message = "…", code = "…")]` is one rule.
The plugin runs in *repeated* mode: every source attribute parses into
an independent record appended to `typed_attrs["validate"]: [Value]`.

Plugin codegens — Rust (`sidex-gen-rs`) and TypeScript (`sidex-gen-ts`) —
consume that array and emit per-rule validators that build a
`ValidationReport` of `(path, code, message)` errors.

Typed attributes for API stability and lifecycle.

Three plugins describe a definition's lifecycle:

- `#[deprecated(...)]` — the item is deprecated; use something else.
- `#[unstable(...)]`   — the item's contract may change between versions.
- `#[since(...)]`      — the version in which the item was introduced.

Each plugin applies to a definition (record / variant / wrapper / opaque /
alias), a record field, a variant case, and a schema. Codegens propagate
the result to the target language (Rust `#[deprecated]`, TSDoc `@deprecated`
/ `@experimental` / `@since`, PEP 702 `@typing.deprecated`, JSON-Schema
`deprecated` keyword) and prepend a stability prelude to the rendered doc
comment.

Typed attribute for declaring an RPC-style request.

Pairs an action / request record with the response type that the
transport delivers on success. Bundles that want to expose request
listings to their generators (Rust action enums, TypeScript clients,
OpenAPI specs, …) read the `request` plugin entry from each def's
`typed_attrs` and walk the resolved response reference.

Source form:

```sidex
#[request(name = "users.CreateUser", response = CreateUserOutput)]
record CreateUserAction {
email: string,
password?: string,
}
```

Streaming variants (server-streaming, client-streaming, bidi) are
intentionally out of scope for v1 — extra `request_stream` /
`response_stream` flags can be added later without breaking the unary
shape.
State management configuration.
Bootstrapping configuration.

Marks a record or variant as the typed schema for a plugin's attributes
at a specific IR position.

# Example

```sidex
#[attrs(plugin = "json", target = "record")]
record JsonRecordAttrs {
rename_all?: RenameFunction,
deny_unknown_fields?: bool,
}
```

# Repeated mode

Setting `repeated` makes every source `#[plugin(...)]` parse into an
independent record appended to `typed_attrs[plugin]: [Value]`, rather
than merging field-by-field into a single record. Useful for plugins
where each attribute represents one logical unit, e.g. one validation
rule per `#[validate(...)]`:

```sidex
#[attrs(plugin = "validate", target = "field", repeated)]
record FieldRule {
expr: Tokens,
message?: string,
code?: string,
}
```

Plugin identifier — matches the outer path of the source attribute
(e.g., `"json"` matches `#[json(...)]`).

IR position this schema applies to. The compiler routes source
attributes to schemas by `(plugin, target)`.

When set, every source `#[plugin(...)]` parses into an independent
record appended to `typed_attrs[plugin]: [Value]` rather than
merging field-by-field.
IR positions that can carry attributes.
Schema-level: `#[plugin(...)]` at the top of a `.sidex` file.
Any top-level definition, regardless of kind.
A field of a record type.
A case of a variant type.

Marks a `bool`-typed field that may be set by bare-path presence
(e.g., `#[plugin(deny_unknown_fields)]` ↔ `deny_unknown_fields = true`).

Marks a `string`-typed field whose source value comes from an
`AttrValue::Path`, not an `AttrValue::String`. Use this when the attribute
looks like `key = some_path` rather than `key = "string"`.

Overrides the field-to-attribute name mapping. By default the field name
is the attribute name; use `#[attr(name = "...")]` to differ.

A resolved reference to a Sidex definition, carried as the value of an
attribute field.

When a plugin's attribute schema declares a field of type `TypeRef`, the
compiler resolves the source path through the same name-resolution
machinery as type expressions and stores the resulting `DefRef` on the
IR node's `typed_attrs`. This enables patterns like:

```ignore
#[request(response = ResponseType)]
```

where the codegen consuming the IR sees `response` as a fully-qualified
reference into the IR's `defs` arena.

A captured `{ … }`-form attribute value.

When a plugin's attribute schema declares a field of type `Tokens`, the
compiler captures the verbatim source text between the braces along
with its span and stores the result on the IR node's `typed_attrs` as a
JSON object `{ "text": "...", "span": { ... } }`. The plugin parses the
text with its own grammar — e.g. the validation extension's predicate
expressions:

```ignore
#[validate({ 0 <= _ <= 100 }, message = "Out of range.")]
```
A sequence of Unicode code points.
A sequence of bytes.
An 8-bit signed integer
A 16-bit signed integer.
A 32-bit signed integer.
A 64-bit signed integer.
An 8-bit unsigned integer.
A 16-bit unsigned integer.
A 32-bit unsigned integer.
A 64-bit unsigned integer.

Type used for indexing sequences.

This is an unsigned integer of target-specific bit width.
A 32-bit floating-point number.
A 64-bit floating-point number.
A boolean.
Unit type indicating the absence of any specific data.
A sequence type.
A map type.

Python-specific attributes that may be applied to an `opaque` definition.

Source form: `#[py(typ = "module.Type")]`. The `typ` value is the
fully-qualified Python type path that the codegen emits as a type alias.
The Python type path (e.g. `"datetime.datetime"`).

JSON-specific attributes that may be applied to an `opaque` definition.

Source form: `#[json(type = "string|null", schema = "/path")]`.
JSON union type expression (e.g. `"string|null"`).
JSON Schema path emitted as `$ref` for the opaque type.

JSON-specific attributes that may be applied to a `record` definition.

Source form: `#[json(rename_all = "PascalCase")]`.
Default rename function applied to every field name.

JSON-specific attributes that may be applied to a record `field`.

Source form: `#[json(name = "explicit", rename = "camelCase", inline)]`.
Override the JSON key with an explicit string.
Apply a rename function to the field name.
Whether to inline the field's contents into the enclosing object.

JSON-specific attributes that may be applied to a `variant` definition.

Source form:
`#[json(rename_all = "snake_case", tagged = internally, tag = "kind")]`.
Default rename function applied to every variant case name.

Tagging strategy: `adjacently`, `externally`, `internally`,
or `implicitly`.

Field name carrying the tag value (for `adjacently` /
`internally` tagging modes). Defaults to `"tag"`.

Field name carrying the variant's payload (for `adjacently`
tagging mode). Defaults to `"content"`.

JSON-specific attributes that may be applied to a single variant case.

Source form: `#[json(name = "explicit", rename = "kebab-case")]`.
Override the JSON tag value with an explicit string.
Apply a rename function to the case name.

Override the per-case content field name (only meaningful with
`adjacently` tagging).
Restriction on a `pub` visibility — `pub(crate)` or `pub(super)`.
`#[rust(pub(crate))]` — restrict to the current crate.
`#[rust(pub(super))]` — restrict to the parent module.
Rust attributes that may apply to any definition.

`#[rust(type = "::path::To::Type")]` — emit a type alias to the
referenced Rust type instead of generating one.

`#[rust(derive(Clone, Debug, ...))]` — extra derives applied to the
generated type. Multiple occurrences accumulate.

`#[rust(attr(non_exhaustive))]` — extra Rust attributes applied to
the generated type. Multiple occurrences accumulate; nested lists
(e.g. `attr(serde(transparent))`) are stringified verbatim.
Rust attributes that may apply to a record `field`.
`#[rust(name = "explicit")]` — override the Rust identifier.
`#[rust(pub)]` (default) plus `pub(crate)` / `pub(super)` variants.

`#[rust(private)]` — explicitly private (the default for fields, so
this is mostly used to override a record-level visibility).
`#[rust(box)]` — wrap the field type in `::std::boxed::Box`.
`#[rust(arc)]` — wrap the field type in `::std::sync::Arc`.
`#[rust(rc)]` — wrap the field type in `::std::rc::Rc`.

`#[rust(wrap = "::path::To::Type")]` — wrap the field type in a
custom Rust type.

One validation rule attached to a field.

Source form:

```sidex
#[validate({ 0 <= _ <= 100 })]
#[validate({ 1 <= _.size }, message = "Required.")]
#[validate({ matches(_, "...") }, message = "...", code = "format:phone")]
```

The rule expression captured verbatim. Plugins parse this body with
the validation expression grammar.

Optional human-readable message. When unset, the runtime picks a
default keyed on the resolved error code (e.g. `"Value must be at
least 1 character."` for `min_length`).

Override the error code emitted by recognized shapes (e.g. surface
a `regex` rule as `"format:phone"`).

One validation rule attached to a record. The expression sees the whole
record as `_`, with field accessors (`_.<field>`) for cross-field rules.

One validation rule attached to a wrapper. The expression sees the
wrapped value as `_`. Codegens may emit `try_new` constructors that
run validation up front when the wrapper has rules.

One validation rule attached to an opaque type. The expression sees the
opaque value as `_`. Useful for primitive opaques whose representation
is a string or number with target-specific semantics.

`#[deprecated]` on a top-level definition.

Source forms:

```sidex
#[deprecated]
#[deprecated(since = "2.3.0", note = "Use `NewThing`.")]
#[deprecated(since = "2.3.0", remove_in = "4.0.0", note = "Use `NewThing`.")]
```

`since` and `remove_in` are free-form version strings (SemVer by
convention); `note` is human-readable migration guidance.
Version in which the item was deprecated.
Migration guidance — what to use instead.

Version in which the item is scheduled to be removed. While the
current bundle version is below `remove_in`, tooling emits a
deprecation warning; once the bundle version reaches `remove_in`
and the item is still present, the warning is escalated to an error.
`#[deprecated]` on a record field.
`#[deprecated]` on a variant case.
`#[deprecated]` at the top of a schema file.

`#[unstable]` on a top-level definition.

Source forms:

```sidex
#[unstable]
#[unstable(feature = "tags", note = "Field set still in flux.",
issue = "https://github.com/silitics/sidex/issues/42")]
```

Optional feature-gate identifier. Lets a bundle expose multiple
unstable surfaces independently; consumers can opt in granularly.
Human-readable rationale — what's still in flux and why.
Optional URL to a tracking issue / RFC.
`#[unstable]` on a record field.
`#[unstable]` on a variant case.
`#[unstable]` at the top of a schema file.

`#[since]` on a top-level definition.

Source form:

```sidex
#[since(version = "1.4.0")]
```

`version` is per-item: `since` on a field means "this field appeared in
version X" and is independent of the enclosing def's `since`.

Version in which the item was introduced. Free-form; SemVer by
convention.
`#[since]` on a record field.
`#[since]` on a variant case.
`#[since]` at the top of a schema file.
One RPC request declaration attached to a record.

Wire-level identifier — what client and server agree on as the
stable name for this request. Free-form; senders may wrap it into
any namespacing convention (`users.CreateUser`,
`users_CreateUser`, …).

Response record the request resolves to on success. Must reference
a record / variant / wrapper / opaque def reachable from the
bundle that uses this attribute.
Rugix Apps configuration.

The service manager available on the system (e.g., `"systemd"`).

Defaults to `"none"`.
Persisted app state.
No generation is active.
A transition is in progress.
A generation is active.
The workload is being started.
The workload is being stopped.
A transition and automatic rollback both failed.
Details of a starting state.
The active generation number.
Details of a stopping state.
The active generation number.
Details of a switching state.
Generation being deactivated, if any.
Generation being activated, if any.
Whether this switch is a recovery attempt.
Details of an active state.
The active generation number.
Details of an error state.
The generation that was previously active before the failed switch, if any.
The generation that failed to activate.
Human-readable error description.
Live workload status.
The workload is running and healthy.
The workload is running but failing health checks.
The workload is not running.
The workload has failed.
The status could not be determined.
Details for a status with a message.
Human-readable description.
Metadata for a single generation, persisted in `.rugix/generation.json`.
Generation number.
Creation timestamp (RFC 3339).
Timestamp of the last successful activation (RFC 3339), if any.
System configuration.
Config partition configuration.
Data partition configuration.
System slots.
System boot groups.
Boot flow configuration.
Partition configuration.
Explicitly disable the partition.
Path to the partition block device.
Partition number of the root device.

User-defined script for mounting the partition.

Mutually exclusive with `driver`.
Path where the partition is or should be mounted.
Indicates whether the partition is write-protected.

Driver responsible for formatting, mounting, and wiping the partition.

Only valid on the data partition. Mutually exclusive with `mount_script`.
When unset, the partition is treated as a plain Ext4 filesystem.

Data partition driver configuration.

The driver owns the data partition's lifecycle: bootstrap-time format,
per-boot mount, and cryptographic erasure on wipe. When unset, the
partition is treated as a plain Ext4 filesystem.
Plain Ext4 filesystem on the bare partition device.

LUKS2 with a passphrase loaded from a file at unlock time.

**Not** a stand-alone production encryption mode: a passphrase living
on plaintext flash defeats the encryption. Useful for testing and
for out-of-band-delivered keys never persisted locally.

LUKS2 with a key sealed by a TPM 2.0 device.

`systemd-cryptenroll` seals a random LUKS2 passphrase to the TPM at
format time; `cryptsetup open --token-only` unseals it on every boot.
Production-grade: the unseal material never leaves the TPM.
User-defined driver implemented by external scripts.
Configuration for the plain Ext4 driver.
Filesystem label assigned at format time. Defaults to `data`.
Additional options forwarded verbatim to `mkfs.ext4`.
Configuration for the LUKS2 passphrase driver.
Path to the file holding the passphrase. Read raw, used as-is.
Filesystem label assigned at mkfs time. Defaults to `data`.
Name of the dm-crypt mapper device. Defaults to `rugix-data`.
Additional options forwarded verbatim to `mkfs.ext4`.
Configuration for the LUKS2 TPM2 driver.

PCRs to bind the unseal policy to. Empty list means no PCR binding.

**Update warning:** PCRs in the 0-9 range typically change with
firmware/bootloader/kernel updates and will brick the device on
every update if bound literally. PCR 7 (Secure Boot policy state)
is the most stable choice for static binding. For stronger binding,
future revisions will add `tpm2-public-key` for `TPM2_PolicyAuthorize`
so PCR predictions can be re-signed per release.
TPM 2.0 device path. Defaults to `auto`.
Filesystem label assigned at mkfs time. Defaults to `data`.
Name of the dm-crypt mapper device. Defaults to `rugix-data`.
Additional options forwarded verbatim to `mkfs.ext4`.

Configuration for the custom driver. Each script receives the device
path as `$1`, the mount point as `$2`, and the environment variables
`RUGIX_DATA_DEVICE` and `RUGIX_DATA_MOUNT_POINT`.
Bootstrap-time format script.
Per-boot mount script.
`rugix-ctrl data wipe` script.
System slot configuration.
Block device slot.
File slot.
Custom slot.
Block device slot configuration.
Path to the block device.
Partition number of the block device.
If true, the slot is allowed to be absent.
File slot configuration.
Custom slot configuration.
Boot group configuration.
Slot aliases of the boot group.
Boot flow configuration
Tryboot boot flow.
U-Boot boot flow.
Generic U-boot boot flow.
Grub (EFI) boot flow.
RAUC-compatible U-Boot boot flow.
RAUC-compatible Grub boot flow.
Mender-compatible Grub boot flow.
Mender-compatible U-Boot boot flow.

Systemd-boot boot flow.

Uses EFI variables (LoaderEntryDefault, LoaderEntryOneShot) to control
which boot entry is selected by systemd-boot. Requires a mapping from
boot group names to systemd-boot entry IDs.
Custom, user-defined boot flow.
Systemd-boot boot flow configuration.

Mapping from boot group names to systemd-boot entry IDs.

Example: `{ a = "nixos-a.efi", b = "nixos-b.efi" }`
RAUC boot flow configuration.

RAUC boot group names.

Defaults to the respective Rugix boot group names converted to uppercase.

Default number of attempts left when committing or marking a boot group as good.

Defaults to 3.

Note that this is only used by U-Boot (and potentially Barebox in the future).
Mender boot flow configuration.

Directory of the Mender boot partition.

Defaults to `/boot`.

Boot Partition A.

Defaults to 2.

Boot partition B.

Defaults to 3.
Custom boot flow configuration.
Path to the script implementing the boot flow.
State management configuration.
Configuration of the root overlay.
Files and directories to persist.
Configuration of the root overlay.
Put the overlay on the data partition and persist it across boots.
Put the overlay on the data partition and discard it on each boot.
Put the overlay in a temporary, in-memory filesystem.
Disable the overlay.
Configuration to persist a file or directory.
Configuration to persist a file.
Configuration to persist a directory.
Configuration to persist a file.
Path of the file to persist.
Default contents to initialize the file with if it does not exist.
Configuration to persist a directory.
Path of the directory to persist.
Bootstrapping configuration.
Disable bootstrapping altogether.
System layout configuration to use for bootstrapping.
System layout configuration.
MBR partition layout.
GPT partition layout.
Default partition layout.
No partition layout.
Partition layout configuration.
Partitions of the layout.
Configuration of a partition of a layout.
Optional name of the partition.
Size of the partition.
Type of the partition (one byte hex or GUID).
Filesystem of the partition.
Default layout configuration.
Size of the system partitions.
Events emitted by CLI tools.
Emitted when the update progress changes.
Event emitted when the update progress changes.
The update progress.
Rugix Ctrl configuration.
Signature check configuration.

Paths to root certificates.

Currently, only a single root certificate is supported.
Information about the system.
Information about the slots.
Information about the boot flow.
Information about the state management mechanism.
Information about the state management mechanism.
State management is disabled.
State management is active.

State management is inactive due to an error.

State is stored in memory and will fail to persist even if declared.
Information about the state management mechanism, if state management is active.
Device backing the data partition, if any.
Information about a slot.
Indicates whether the slot is active, i.e., in use.
Hashes of the slot data according to the slot database.
Size of the slot data according to the slot database.
Last time the slot has been updated according to the slot database.
Information about the boot flow.
Name of the boot flow.
Active boot group.
Default boot group.
Information about the boot groups.
Information about a boot group.
Information about a block device.
Path of the block device in `/dev`.
Parent of the block device, if any.
Partition number, if the block device is a partition.
Entry in the app list output.
Live workload status.
Currently active generation number, if any.
Metadata of the active generation, if any.
Output of the app info command (`apps info`).
App name.
Live workload status.
Persisted app state.
All generations of the app.
Information about a generation.
Generation number.
Creation timestamp (RFC 3339).
Whether the generation is complete (fully installed).
Timestamp of the last successful activation (RFC 3339), if any.
Whether this is the currently active generation.
User-supplied metadata from `app-meta.json`, if present.
Garbage collection result for a single app.
Generation numbers that were removed.
